text: Here we describe a case for laparoscopic myomectomy for a degenerated posterior wall lower segment uterine fibroid.
The surgeon dissects the broad ligament peritoneum from the posterior aspect of the uterus, exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
After lateralizing the peritoneum, the surgeon visually identifies the degenerated fibroid located on the posterior wall of the lower segment of the uterus.
The surgeon infiltrates vasopressin, diluted in normal saline, into the myometrial wall adjacent to the identified fibroid, preparing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon makes an incision over the myometrium using cold knife scissors, exposing the fibroid beneath without the use of electrocautery.

The surgeon attempts to manipulate the degenerated uterine fibroid using a myoma screw but encounters difficulty. Instead, the fibroid is removed by applying traction and counter traction with needle holders.
The cut end of the uterine musculature shows minimal bleeding due to prior vasopressin infiltration, with the fibroid visible adjacent to the incision site.
The surgeon performs laparoscopic suturing at the posterior lower end of the fibroid, facing challenges due to the needle's angulation in this area.
The surgeon uses figure-of-eight sutures with vicryl to close the incision, opting for this technique due to the fibroid's large sub-serosal component.
The surgeon uses a T-lift device to suspend the ovaries, with the left ovary visibly elevated and held in position.
The T-lift device is used to suspend the ovary, preventing it from dangling and obstructing the surgical field during suturing.
The surgeon uses a three-dimensional laparoscope to enhance depth perception while suturing a low-lying posterior fibroid deep in the pelvis.

Our goal in such cases is to finish off the laparoscopic suturing before the effect of the vasopress in vanes of which is typically approximately forty to forty-five minutes from the time of initial infiltration into the uterine fibroids.
The surgeon uses a MorSafe bag to morcellate the myoma, extracting it from the abdominal cavity.
